sentences.text: Well, so far, aside from the children, which is the most horrible thing of anytime.
Anytime something happens to children, it's the worst thing, of course.
But then I started to talk about the artistic performances.
There were so many others.
There were variety shows.
There were concerts.
There were-- with all these artists there, there were-- there was so much going on.
It looks like it's a wonderful place to be.
Yet you have to see the other side of the, of the story when, when I tell you that, for instance, old folks, and of course, the children, lived in dungeons.
And with that little bit of food and the sicknesses that was going wild there, it was absolutely miserable.
I saw some old people in the garbage bins having their hands in the garbage bins until their armpits, and looking around for some piece of old bread.
Sometimes they picked up a piece of green bread that somebody threw away.
And you could see them swallowing the stuff, eating the stuff.
This was a lot of that going on.
So there was that side of the misery, that people died in, in utter misery and starvation.
And so you-- I-- I showed you the musical side of it.
We-- yeah, all important, we received a little bit of extra food being, being in that musical group.
It was usually a little can with pate in there, which was delicious.
But just like two, two soup spoonsful, maybe, a little tiny flat thing there.
But nevertheless, it had some calories.
And it was important.
So this was basically-- and there's much, much more to be talked about this camp.
It was an interesting place in itself.
It was an old, old-- what's the word-- German word is Festung-- a old citadel, so to speak, with, with catacombs underneath it.
Around it was surrounded by a big moat with, with a a drawbridge like the old medieval times.
Well, it was a wall all around it.
And people were very much confined in that  little place.

People would, in a small room maybe, a room by 15 by 15, there would sometimes be 20 people would be put in there like sardines laid out for the night on the floor on some sacks, one next to the other.
So I want to, to be sure that this picture of the artistry does not mislead anybody.
It was the most fiendish thing, a two-sided-- like a coin with two sides, a two-sided, two-sided thing, two-sided thing that was ever developed.
Yeah.
Theresienstadt, we-- allow me to interject here, too.
Because part of the fiendish thing is also that Hitler allowed a couple of delegations to come.
Once was the Red Cross.
And another one was the King of Denmark.

Had about 500 Jews in there, and he was pestering Hitler so much that finally he allowed-- that Hitler finally allowed-- or Himmler, whoever would be at that time the commander of the-- finally allowed to have a, a Danish delegation come to that camp.
And just-- this is an important scene.
I think I have to point that out.
I was called out during the night to help setting up this thing.
There was the big marketplace.
We were sent to the warehouse.
I was listed apparently as a handy fellow.

And we, out of the big garage, or the big barn out there, there were dismantled parts of a beautiful, white lacquered barrack that we had to bring out into the camp, assemble it-- with red shutters, with a beautiful shiny red roof.
It looked like a little building now out of the fairy tale.
And it was the nursery.
Small children out of the camp would be dressed, well-bathed, and dressed well, and would be put into this thing.
The shelves were full with all kinds of children toys and books.
It happened to be that the Danish delegation came to it-- and before, the Red Cross was the same thing, by the way.
Eventually the Danish delegation that came through happened to be around lunchtime.
The most wonderful food was served to the kids.
The kids had dessert and chocolates.

And the kids were prompted to say, in front of this delegation, and of course the Nazi dignitaries with them, oh, to, to the nurses that were dressed in white uniforms, oh, Miss So-and-So, can, can I, can I have another piece of chocolate, please?
Of course.
Go right over there.
They're in the shelf.
Go, take whatever you want.
And you saw this?
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Taken in the-- that's what we built up.
And so-- and on the, on the same corner of the marketplace, there was-- we had to create, we had to take out the tables like a French outdoor cafe scene.
There was little round tables with those little wrought iron chairs.
There they selected special, fairly good-looking, older folks yet.
And they put them onto those places.
And the corner was made out like a restaurant, building corner.
These people set out there.
And there were served-- if they wanted [? a ?] coffee, or hot chocolate, and wonderful torte pieces.
So they, they were all, all treated to this while the delegation was there.
Of course, when the delegation came home, they reported, yeah, well-- they were allowed to talk to the Danish and to Red Cross, to the prisoners, of course.
The Danish people, prisoners wouldn't dare to talk, because the Nazi uniform-- Nazi staff stood around who understood Danish.
And they knew that, so they would-- could not say, say anything critical.
And so of course, Danish, as well as the Red Cross delegation, came home and said, well, while these people are confined, yes, they cannot leave this place.
But they are treated well.
The kids we see are being treated well, and so on and so forth.
And then--
They bought it.

In fact, after the Red Cross visited, was the German newspapers headlined, while talking about the delegation, the Red Cross delegation that came to there, they headlined, quote, unquote, 'while, while our, while our soldiers on the front are bleeding to death, the Jews are feeding themselves on chocolate drinks and, and, and, tarts.' That was swallowed all the way along.
And then the following night, we had to dismantle that stuff again.
The kids were sent to the dungeons, and the old folks back to their miserable places.
Yeah, but, but you can't let it-- if you want to survive, you cannot let it consume you.
You were aware of it.
But there was nothing you could do.
You just put it aside.
And that's all there is to it.
We were hoping that some, maybe one of the Danes would be able to tell secretly what's really going on or something.
But it apparently never was possible.
So--
Yeah.
We were ordered to be in the morning, some one morning, to be at the train station at Theresien-- in Theresienstadt, or Terezin as the, Terezin, as the Czechs called it.
We had a chance to say goodbye to our mother, which of course was a bad scene, naturally, because all she had were her two sons.
And so we were down at the train station.
And as we arrived, we see the train put together out of cattle cars, the ones with the sliding doors.
Box cars, some people call them, I guess.
We couldn't see the end or the beginning of it, a huge, huge long train.
And people where there-- numbers of people, hundreds or maybe thousands were already we saw driven into the openings of the, of the wagons.
And eventually before we knew it, we were ourselves in there, beaten by gun butts, the guards.
And to help us, we would-- they would drive us in.
And we finally, we sat down.
But we had to get up, because more and more people were driven in.
And because otherwise would-- we would be trampled on.
And so we sat down.
And finally, the train was-- the wagon was loaded up.
You stood actually elbow to elbow, people.
Then the door was shut.
Now you've got to imagine now, if you can, what kind of people were in there.
These were all inmates from even longer than we were there.
By that time, we were there longer than a year in, in, in, in this camp.
You could see old folks, young folks, boys, girls, women, men.
And most of them were skinny.
And many, many of them were actually sick, could hardly stand on their feet.
But there was no way to sit down.
Next to us were a couple of women with infants on their arms.
It was quite a sight.
It didn't take too long when some woman-- the train hadn't rolled yet-- when some woman hollered out, hey, I can't stand on my own feet.
And here's this man hanging on me, like he must be dead.
And what can we do?
Well, everybody had to do with himself, really.
So nobody could pay too much attention.

Nobody did for a moment, until a guy, who identified himself to be a doctor-- you wouldn't believe it if you looked at him, he looked like he escaped from Sing Sing, torn dirty clothes like anybody else.
And he also even had those, those eczemas on his face that I tried to describe earlier.
And he shoved himself through the narrow rows.
And he says, well, let me see what I can find out.
And he got-- shoved himself over to this man there that hung there.
And he hollered over to us, and he says, well-- I don't know why he hollered to us, but we were a younger group.
There were a couple of other guys that were standing there.
He hollered and said, yeah, this man is dead.
What do you think we can do?
Well, you know, somebody bang on the door, so they open the door.
And some of the people near the door banged on the door, but it never was opened.
We could never get rid of the body.
This guy just hung, just hung in there.
The people stood so close that he couldn't even slide down to the floor.
And so the doctor says, I gotta talk to you guys.
At the moment we couldn't figure out why.
He shoved himself through.
And he came to us, and he says, I want you to understand something.
You know, we don't know how long we will be in this train.
We haven't even rolled yet.
And we are here already a couple hours.
I don't know how long.
He says, I want you to know-- so sort of like lowly-- and look at this man over there, look at this woman over there, look at this child over there.
I want you to know, these guys are already gone.
And he pointed at-- what-- five or six people.
They're already are in a coma, he says.
They, they will die very soon.
And so what are we going to do?
We will have possibly if we have to stay for any length of time, there are more people that are close to that condition, even.
Well, we actually decided, thinking-- talking it over very callously, because you cannot afford to be soft [INAUDIBLE] if you want to survive.
We said, well, we've got to create some room where we can pile up the bodies, very coolly.
And so we toss out this man to pile him up at the end of the train down below.
And then the train had rolled, three or four other people had died.
And one of them was a child.
I remember.
I'll never forget.
And we started to put up these body stand at the end of the wagon like that.
And it turned out that we were in this [INAUDIBLE] on-- on, on the road for, in this train, on the, on the rails, for about 2 and 1/2 days.
During this time, there was never a piece of bread given to us, never a drop of water.
And if you think that that is the worst thing there is, then you have to find out, as I will tell you, that the worst thing was that there were no toilet facilities.
I told, I told you about old folks, young folks.
Even a healthy person cannot hold out for two and a, two and 1/2 days.
So I don't need to tell you the mess and the stench of the-- particularly at first right away, the sick people, the old people, and children.
You must understand, you had to live with that.
You try to ignore it as much as you could.
And after all, after all, when we were there, when we arrived in two and 1/2 days, it turned to be out-- to be Auschwitz.
By that time, because of nearly one third of the people had been piled up in form-- in form of bodies in that wagon, there was room for some people to sit down.
Nobody dared, with the exception of some people that were near collapsing.
They had no choice.
They sat down, completely--
Yeah.
Completely given up.
And sitting in this kind of mess because they couldn't stand on their feet anymore.
Yeah.
I don't want to talk about the specifics on that.
It's bad enough.
Yeah.
I saw-- there's more thing-- more about children that I can talk, we'll talk about.
So it turns out that the doors were opened.
We [INAUDIBLE] we saw signs, Auschwitz.
And we looked, we looked into the faces of a long line of SS men and their helpers.
We were ordered out of the train.
At least now finally we got some fresh air as the doors were-- doors were opened.
We that could, were able to hop out, hopped out.
And others were laying down there, didn't move.
And we figured it's the best to leave them there, maybe.
And so we were lined up.
And after standing there for a while, the chief there, the guy with his megaphone, goes, well, he says, we understand that because of an oversight, you didn't get any food.
We want you to understand that there is food waiting for you in the camp.
We are now on the entrance ramp of Auschwitz-Birkenau, the long entrance ramp.
You see some pictures of that.
The train is on this side.
The other side is open.
There's-- there were these guys, the, the, the SS.
Their troops were lined up on the other side of the ramp.
And so he says, there's food waiting for you in the camp.
But before we, we can take you into the camp, there are some items that have to be taken care of.
And the faster you will be comply with that, the faster you will do the things that we ask you to do, the sooner you will be in camp and have your food.
So therefore, we want you to understand that whatever we do, if you hurry up, you get your food very soon.
The first thing we have to do is that because of the fact that you have to work from morning to evening, very late and long hours, you-- I, I see that many of you have, have here small children.
And I'm talking about the children of 13 and under, which, which it was at that point.
There were other ages at certain other times.
When I came [INAUDIBLE], it was 13 and under.
We want you to understand that these children will be put into facilities that they will be well taken care of.
For the, for the infants that I see some of you have, there will be nurseries.
For the other kids of, of that age, there will be kindergartens.
And then for, for the kids of 13 and a little under, around that, we have school facilities.
But to be sure, before anything happens at all, we want you to understand that they, they will be passed by a, by a, past a medical team.
And whatever they need for medical treatments and, and attention, they will get.
So therefore, we want you to take a few minutes now and explain this to the children.
Say goodbye to them, and send them over here to the other side of the ramp.
And by the way, don't forget to point out to them that maybe not this weekend, but the next weekend you will be able to visit them.
So you be sure to explain this to the smaller children particularly, so that they will grasp that, and so on.
So take your-- take some time now and do that.
Well, the time was up.
And nothing had happened.
Nothing had moved.
The guy goes to his megaphone, well, listen now, we are not hungry.
We have our food here.
But you guys want to eat, don't you?
So therefore, you better get with it, and so you can get to the camp and get your food, dangling this in front, you know?
And he says, I give you another little time, so that you can explain this and so on and so forth.
So anyways, when that time was up, nothing had happened yet either.
Then after a while, it was one more time, and he says, now look, you guys, we don't want to stand here all day long, either.

So if nothing happens in the next 10 minutes, if you don't have to-- if you haven't brought your kids over here to the other side, our guards will come in and we will force you-- we will separate you forcefully.
Then of course, what happened.
The guards came in and grabbed the kids and shoved them over to the other side.
And some of them hollered, hey, wait a minute.
I'm, I'm 14.
He said 13 age.
And so-- and the guards didn't even pay any attention.
These were big, tall guys.
They took their big boots and kicked the kids over to the other side.
There were these two women standing with infants on their, on their arms.

The one guard came over, grabbed the infant, jerked it out of her arm, and threw it over into the other side of the ramp among the other children that were there already, at which moment, this other woman with the infant on the arm quickly went over to drop the child there.
She stayed there for a while, then she was forced back, too.
Yet I have to say-- and I wonder if kids can imagine it in this respect-- the worst scene that ever-- that I ever saw was the separation of children from their, from their loved ones.
It's an indescribable scene.
And I don't even want to talk much more about that.

The only thing that has to be talked about is when it finally, when kids were finally over there-- and many of them were much older than 13-- eventually, the trucks came, loaded up the kids onto the trucks.
The trucks turned around and went into the directions of the smokestacks that we, that we saw spewing out the smoke, whose stench we smelled of burnt flesh already the moment we got out of the train.
And even some white flakes would fall down on us from the ashes that would, like snow would fall on us.
These-- then the kids were gone.
Then the same thing went, anybody that needs medical attention over here.
And we need you to work and things like that.
And people that went over there to the side and figured, yeah, they need us to work.
So maybe they mean what they say.
Give us medical attention, and even if, if need be, a day or two rest before we send you to work.
Some people believed in that.
And they went over now.
By that time, my brother and I had for a long time had recognized that the only way is to be, to be available to work.
We didn't believe that they would give you any kind of rest or anything like that, although it sounded very, very tantalizing.

And so as people went over there that weren't in any worse shape than we were, in fact, everybody that was there really would have needed medical attention, if it comes right down to it, including ourselves.
But we didn't go for that.
And we knew that our only chance would be survival.
We had heard already then, too, that the Nazis had really were thrown back in many places.
And at that time-- this now I'm talking about beginning of, beginning of October in 1944.
The war wasn't over yet for another seven or eight months.
At that time, we figured already, the war would, would last only maybe a couple of weeks.
That's all you have to hang in there.
So we were always optimistic, always figuring that out, always trying to figure that out.
And so therefore, we said, we got to be available to go to work.
And so finally then the sick people that were loaded up also on the trucks, the trucks went in the same direction as these kids were.
There was one, one person that we knew very well, who wasn't any quote, unquote 'sicker' than we were.
But he hoped that he could be having a day of rest, that, that brings him closer to, to, to the liberation.
And we tried to talk him out of it.
He went with this group.
And we never saw him again.
Ever.
And so then we were finally taken into the camp.
We were ordered to be-- going to single file.
And we are there.
One of the SS men-- in our case, it was not Mengele.
Very often, Mengele did that himself, because he was also looking for people that he could do experiments on.
But in our case, it wasn't there.
It was another high-ranking SS officer who would face us as we, as we approached him.
And he would just go with him thumb, with his thumb, this way, that way.
In other words, there are many people in the single file that were of the same opinion as we were, that you have to get through to, to be chosen to work in order to survive.
But some of them could hardly walk.
You could see.
And of course, these eventually then, the guy went like this.
They went to the, to the Auschwitz side.
And we went to the Birkenau side, which was the work camp of the Auschwitz camp.
And so we were in this camp then.
We, we got in.
By the way, this was, the whole thing took almost all day long.
We got in.
And we started-- we stopped in front of a big barrack, which turned out to be a shower room.
We were ordered to shed our clothes.
And we were taken into that room.
And we had a wonderful shower.
But by the way, the suitcases that we had-- that they took away from us right away when we, when we entered the train, they said, you will get your suitcase afterwards.
It was like as if you go to a spa, you know, where they deliver the suitcase after.
They were taken away.
We never saw that again, of course.
And so now here we were in a shower.
And we were ordered to get out of the shower.
And there was a long line of tables with these prisoner's striped clothes.
And we were moving towards that.
And the guy with his megaphone, oh, wait a minute.
Hold everything.
We are not ready for that yet.
First of all, your hair has to be shorn.
So we stood, had to stand in line, and all our hair was shorn, in fact, all over the body.
And then when this was done, we thought, now we can get our clothes.
He says, no.
Wait a minute.
There is one more thing that we have to do before you get to the camp.
We want you to understand that from now on, you don't use your name anymore.
You don't use your name anymore.
We didn't have anything anymore that we could call our own.
At that moment, you realize that your name is now the most prized possession.
And he wants to take that away from us also.
Just imagine it.
I am asking you your name, and you spurt it out without thinking much about it.
It's your name.
Very rarely do you-- does it have any meaning to you.
You spurt it out without any meaning.
It's, it's an automatic reaction.
You spurt out your name.
But here now all of a sudden, it wasn't that anymore.
It was, oh my gosh.
That's right.
It's our name.
He wants to take that away from us.
And he goes on saying, now therefore, we will assign a number to you that you will use.
Even if we ask you, ask you your name, you will answer with that number.
We want you to understand it and remember this.

Now he says, the number that will be assigned to you, it will be impossible for us to issue you identification cards, which, by the way, even if you would have those-- and he started to laugh-- knowing you guys, you would only lose it anyways.
And he started to laugh.
And his two underlings laughed with him.
They bent over for that, that was a funny crack to them.
And so these cards-- therefore there will be no cards.
Besides, most of the uniforms don't have any pockets anyways.
We have a much, much better idea.
We will make sure that you never forget or lose your number.
We will tattoo them onto your arm.
Now as I say, at this moment, you own nothing else anymore.
You have now lost your name.
That is the ultimate dehumanization.
You think that is the end of the dehumanization.
There is nothing else now anymore.
Now you're in the camp.
And you just hang in there for dear life, literally.
So my number here is right on my left arm here.
It is sort of like upside down when you hold-- when I hold it like this, because it has to be like this.
We had to hold the arm this way that when the SS man looked at it, when you had to recite it, that he could easily read along with it.
And he has it right on there.
So this was it.
Now by the way, there were about some 20 rows of tattooers.

In the row that I was, it happened to be that the guy apparently was neat, because I know a fellow here in Saint Paul that has his number on the outside of his arm, very, very crooked and, and uneven.
Many people ask me, why do you have that thing yet?
Why don't you get rid of it?
I have no reason to get rid of it.
That's not a big operation.
It's a minor skin graft.
It's not a big deal.
But I don't see any reason for it-- nothing to be ashamed of.
So this now, then when this was done, then we were taken into the camp.
And yes, and there the food was there, the bowl of soup and a piece of bread.
But the guy with his megaphone couldn't, couldn't help but making another remark, saying, look you guys.
If you had taken five minutes more, that would have been taken all the way.
And you would have had nothing to eat until tomorrow morning-- the black coffee, the black water.
So this was our arrival in Auschwitz.
We were then, as I say, in that barracks.
And the next morning, we, we were assigned to work groups.
And again, there too, I was fortunate enough to be ordered into a, into a workshop, where they assigned my own guard.
And I marched and had my own guard.
I felt like a big shot.
Of course, the interesting thing is, this guard wasn't there to, to protect me.
He was guarding that I don't do anything bad, the most dangerous fellow that I was.
My brother and I fortunately were still together at that moment.
We were separated later on.
And I'll probably come to that somewhat later.
Our mother remained in, in, in Terezin.

And when-- what we heard from eyewitnesses, when she was becoming more and more desperate, she went to the officials and asked them, in her naivete-- and I, I mean this lovingly-- and asked them that she would be, that she, if she couldn't be sent to her sons.
After all, that's what a mother wants to do then.
And so of course, they were always welcoming volunteers that would be sent away.
And so therefore, she was put on train.
From witnesses that were with her, we found out that she did the same train ride.
And it's, it's a miracle that she survived it, because by that time, she was a gravely ill woman.
Number one, she had a very serious cancer surgery from which she had not yet recovered when she was, when we were incarcerated.
She was incarcerated.
Number two, she was a diabetic that needed insulin, which of course, in the camps, you never received.

And then of course-- again, I mean this lovingly-- as gullible as she was, when these people offered her medical help-- and she arrived the same station, the same that I described, the same scene-- when they asked for sick people that should be-- that, that are needed to work, come over here for treatment.
She, of course, went over there.
And by that time, she-- witnesses told us that she was gravely, gravely ill, and very weak.
And so she hoped, of course, that they meant it.
In any case, these sick people were loaded up onto the trucks like the children and went in the same direction.
And this is what we heard from people who with her to that moment.
A person that we met just by coincidence-- I don't know the name anymore.
Almost, almost immediately afterwards in, in Munich.
Yeah.
